summary.brief: Dierl v. Birkin. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: Dierl was injured at a ski resort in Park City in February 2015 and was taken by ambulance to an emergency room.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Birkin, a radiologist, interpreted Dierl’s CT brain scan and reported it as a '[n]egative CT,' stating that '[t]here are no intracranial masses.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Beginning in November 2015, Dierl began experiencing 'severe headaches, loss of balance, dizziness, speech impediment, memory loss[,] and loss of vision.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The doctor 'informed Dierl that he had a large tumor in his brain and that was the reason for his suffering and loss of vision.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In reality, the scan showed a tumor in Dierl’s brain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He suffered complications associated with the surgery, including permanent partial loss of vision and pituitary gland damage.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court granted summary judgment to Birkin because Dierl offered no admissible expert testimony establishing that Birkin’s failure to diagnose Dierl’s brain tumor nine months earlier proximately caused Dierl’s injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Neurosurgeon 1 testified that the tumor grew by four millimeters—from twenty to twenty-four millimeters—over the course of the nine months following the February 2015 CT scan.
evidence_index.speaker: Neurosurgeon 1

evidence_index.text: Neurosurgeon 2 had 'determined to a medical degree of certainty . . . [t]hat the failure of [Birkin] to diagnose the head CT scan and report the tumor in . . . Dierl’s brain on February 12, 2015[,] allowed the tumor to enlarge.'
evidence_index.speaker: Neurosurgeon 2

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, the court granted summary judgment in Birkin’s favor and dismissed Dierl’s claim with prejudice.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The district court properly exercised its discretion in excluding the affidavits of both experts.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was injured at a ski resort and taken by ambulance to an emergency room. A CT scan of the brain was performed.

requestables.name: CT Brain Scan
requestables.description: CT scan of the brain to assess for intracranial injury

reveal.result_summary: Negative CT reported by radiologist, but actually showed a brain tumor
reveal.clinical_significance: Misinterpretation led to delayed diagnosis of brain tumor
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted further investigation or referral

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED after ski injury

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in emergency room with head injury
decision_points.prompt: How should the CT brain scan be interpreted?

options.description: Report CT scan as showing a brain tumor

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant reported CT scan as negative
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant stated there were no intracranial masses

expected_action_court.description: CT scan should have been reported as showing a brain tumor
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated tumor was visible

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: The CT scan actually showed a tumor, which was not reported
explanation.what_should_have_happened: The tumor should have been identified and reported
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care requires accurate interpretation of imaging

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed severe symptoms due to tumor growth, requiring surgery
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Permanent partial loss of vision and pituitary gland damage

key_findings: Summary judgment granted due to lack of admissible expert testimony on causation

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to diagnose brain tumor on CT scan
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Earlier diagnosis could have led to earlier treatment

factual_timeline.event: Patient injured at ski resort and taken to emergency room
Patient underwent surgery for brain tumor

complications.complication: Permanent partial loss of vision and pituitary gland damage

court_findings.finding: No admissible expert testimony on causation
